did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Deep Learning (CNN, VGG16) and Machine Learning (SVM, Random Forest) models to classify skin lesions from the HAM10000 dataset, comparing their performance on original versus FMM-compressed images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from classifying skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset with Deep Learning (VGG16, CNN) and Machine Learning (SVM, Random Forest) models on both original and Five Modulus Method (FMM) compressed images. It includes a methodology section detailing dataset preprocessing, compression algorithm application, and model training, followed by a results section with accuracy tables and PSNR analysis, concluding with findings on model performance. Although published in a conference proceedings (CONECCT), it reports new empirical findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Classification of Skin Lesions using Five Modulus Method for image compression
journal: 2024 IEEE International Conference on Electronics, Computing and Communication Technologies (CONECCT)
brief_summary: This study evaluated the impact of Five Modulus Method (FMM) image compression on the classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The researchers applied FMM compression to dermoscopic images and trained both deep learning models (VGG16, CNN) and machine learning models (SVM, Random Forest) on the compressed data. Performance was assessed by comparing accuracy metrics against models trained on original uncompressed images, while Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) was used to verify image quality retention. The results indicated that FMM compression preserved essential information, with PSNR values ranging from 43dB to 50dB. Consequently, there was minimal difference in classification accuracy between the compressed and original datasets across all models. The study concluded that deep learning models outperformed machine learning models, and FMM compression is viable for reducing storage without significantly compromising diagnostic performance.

all_task_types.target: Seven different types of skin lesions (Melanocytic nevi, melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratoses, vascular lesions, dermatofibroma, and benign keratosis-like lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. Models (VGG16, CNN, SVM, Random Forest) predict discrete diagnostic categories (lesion classes) from both original and Five Modulus Method (FMM) compressed images. Performance is evaluated using accuracy metrics.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A diverse collection of dermoscopic images of pigmented lesions from multiple sources, containing metadata such as age, gender, and localization. It was the primary dataset used in the study.

use.training: Used for training deep learning (VGG16, CNN) and machine learning (SVM, Random Forest) models on both original and Five Modulus Method (FMM) compressed images.
use.testing: Used for testing via three different train-test split ratios (80-20, 70-30, 60-40) to evaluate model accuracy.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To analyze the quality of compressed images and compare them with original images as a similarity measure.
To compute the difference between original and compressed images, serving as a component for PSNR calculation.
To evaluate model performance and ensure robustness by testing on distinct training and testing sets across multiple trials.
To assess the difference in classification performance between models trained on original versus FMM-compressed images.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance differences between original and compressed images were described qualitatively as 'minimal difference' or 'extremely little variation' based on accuracy metrics and PSNR values.

HAM10000.notes: PSNR values for compressed images ranged from 43dB to 50dB. The paper notes minimal difference in accuracy between compressed and original images across all models and trials. Deep learning models (CNN, VGG16) outperformed machine learning models (SVM, Random Forest).

architecture_details.details: Deep learning models (VGG16, CNN) and machine learning models (SVM, Random Forest) were used for classification. The paper does not specify the exact CNN architecture details (e.g., layer count, filter sizes) beyond the name 'CNN'. VGG16 is referenced by name. SVM and Random Forest are standard machine learning classifiers.

training_details.details: Models were trained on both original and Five Modulus Method (FMM) compressed images. Pre-processing included converting to RGB, normalizing pixel values, and resizing to a standard resolution. Hyperparameter optimization techniques were mentioned as being used for a fair comparison, but specific values are not reported.

classification_or_output_head.details: Seven classes: Melanocytic nevi, melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratoses, vascular lesions, dermatofibroma, and benign keratosis-like lesions.

preprocessing_techniques: Converting images to RGB color system
Normalizing pixel values to a common range
Resizing images to a standard resolution

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper did not report using any data augmentation techniques (such as geometric transforms, color transforms, or synthetic data generation) for the HAM10000 dataset in their own experiments. The study focused on the impact of Five Modulus Method (FMM) image compression on classification accuracy. While the introduction mentions a separate study [1] that used DCGANs for dataset balancing, this was not part of the current paper's methodology.

conclusions: The Five Modulus Method (FMM) image compression technique effectively preserves essential visual information and diagnostic features of dermoscopic skin lesion images, as evidenced by Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) values ranging from 43dB to 50dB.

There is minimal difference in classification accuracy between models trained on original uncompressed images and those trained on FMM-compressed images across all tested models and train-test split ratios (80-20, 70-30, 60-40).
Deep learning models (VGG16 and CNN) outperformed machine learning models (SVM and Random Forest) in classifying skin lesions from the HAM10000 dataset.
FMM compression is a viable method for reducing storage space and computational load for skin lesion images without significantly compromising the performance of automated diagnostic systems.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the lack of clinical expert validation and suggests future work should involve collaboration with clinical experts and qualitative assessments by medical professionals to align methodologies with practical clinical needs.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation, relying solely on the HAM10000 dataset without testing on independent clinical cohorts.
There is no qualitative assessment by dermatologists or clinical experts to verify if the preserved visual features are diagnostically sufficient.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the observed accuracy differences between compressed and original images.
Specific hyperparameters for the CNN architecture and optimization techniques are not detailed, limiting reproducibility.
The dataset class distribution is imbalanced (as noted in Figure 3 description), which may bias model performance, particularly for underrepresented classes like melanoma.
The study acknowledges that FMM is a lossy compression method, implying potential information loss that was not quantitatively linked to diagnostic error rates.
No data leakage risks were explicitly addressed, though standard train-test splits were used.
The paper is a conference proceeding with limited scope, focusing primarily on storage efficiency rather than clinical diagnostic robustness.
